--- title: "" format: revealjs: slide-level: 3 link-external-newwindow: true chalkboard: true controls: true auto-stretch: false code-overflow: wrap tbl-colwidths: [50,25,25] --- # Hello Again! {.smaller} [**Please complete this check-in as a warm-up!**](https://docs.google.com/forms/d/e/1FAIpQLSdUuPUWN5_lkHw8DkudwT0--qu8eEFurLSYeJ227V37LC3g1A/viewform?usp=header) ::::: columns ::: {.column width="60%"} **Use the [hormone dataset](https://www.dropbox.com/scl/fo/2f7kaifml4sq8mq1c7dfi/ALPLJarvk4vomfrddx2Buyg?rlkey=i62g4s3nzeldyvvffgviolobi&dl=0) to...** - DESCRIBE : what is the mean of narc_score & age? the % of male and female participants? - PREDICT : are there differences in the level of narcissism for males and females? why? - ERROR : how good is this prediction? do you believe in this pattern? why? - CONTROL : how might we use this knowledge? what other questions do you have? ::: ::: {.column width="40%"} **Description of Variables** - age = age of the participant (in years) - narc_score = a measure of narcissism (how much the person thinks they are better than others) - sex = biological sex (1 = male, 2 = female) ::: ::::: ### ACTIVITY : count the number of times the basketball is passed {{< video https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=vJG698U2Mvo&t=6s width="100%" height = "100%" >}} ## Lab 1 Review. ### DISCUSS: The Monkey In Lab 1 {.smaller} - did you see the gorilla in the data? - reactions to the gorilla? - how did you answer the question? \[linear model, comparison of means, etc?\] ![](images/clipboard-3307213162.png) ### [RESEARCH](https://genomebiology.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13059-020-02133-w) ::: r-fit-text *When people were given a hypothesis, they were less likely to see the gorilla in the data then when left to "explore" the dataset on their own.* ![](images/clipboard-1333648005.png){fig-align="center" width="70%"} ::: ### Does AI See the Gorilla? [Blog Post from February 2025](https://chiraaggohel.com/posts/llms-eda/index.html) ![](images/clipboard-562719887.png) ### Does AI See the Gorilla? [Blog Post from March 2025](https://www.hudsong.dev/ai-gorilla-pattern-detection) ![](images/clipboard-3703370188.png){fig-align="center" width="50%"} # PART 1 : The Linear Model (It's All Lines) ### Check-In (And Linear Model) Review {.smaller} ::::::::: panel-tabset #### Description (Number) ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} par(mfrow = c(1,2)) wrongage <- h$age hist(wrongage) h$age[h$age < 0] <- NA hist(h$age) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](3_xeboot_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-2-1.png){width=672} ::: ```{.r .cell-code} knitr::kable(cbind(mean = mean(wrongage, na.rm = T), mean = mean(h$age, na.rm = T))) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} | mean| mean| |--------:|--------:| | 19.53279| 27.85088| ::: ::: #### Description (Category) ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} h$sexF <- as.factor(h$sex) levels(h$sexF) <- c("Male", "Female") h$sexF <- relevel(h$sexF, ref = "Female") plot(h$sexF) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](3_xeboot_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-3-1.png){width=672} ::: ```{.r .cell-code} summary(h$sexF) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Female Male 35 87 ``` ::: ::: #### Predict : A Model ::::: columns ::: {.column width="40%"} ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} ggplot(h, aes(x = sexF, y = narc_scale)) + stat_summary(fun = mean, geom = "bar", width = .2) + geom_smooth(method = "lm", aes(group = 1), se = FALSE) + coord_cartesian(ylim = c(1, 5)) + xlab("Sex of Participant") + ylab("Narcissism Score (1-5 Scale)") + ggthemes::theme_base() ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](3_xeboot_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-4-1.png){fig-align='center' width=384} ::: ::: ::: ::: {.column width="60%"} ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} library(jtools) mod1 <- lm(narc_scale ~ sexF, data = h) export_summs(mod1) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
Model 1
(Intercept)3.01 ***
(0.09)   
sexFMale0.26 *  
(0.11)   
N114       
R20.05    
*** p < 0.001; ** p < 0.01; * p < 0.05.
``` ::: ::: ::: ::::: #### Predict : The "Four Reasons" Which of these came to mind? Which are relevant? 1. Causation 2. Reverse Causation 3. Confound Variables 4. Chance (next class!) *Note : Correlation is not causation but causation will be correlated :)* #### To Define Error. ::::: columns ::: {.column width="50%"} ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} par(mfrow = c(1,2)) plot(h$narc_scale, pch = 19, main = "Black = The Mean") abline(h = mean(h$narc_scale, na.rm = T), lwd = 5) plot(h$narc_scale, col = h$sexF, pch = 19, main = "Black = Female; Red = Male") abline(h = coef(mod1)[1], col = 'black', lwd = 5) abline(h = coef(mod1)[1] + coef(mod1)[2], col = 'red', lwd = 5) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](3_xeboot_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-6-1.png){width=768} ::: ::: ::: ::: {.column width="50%"} $$ \hat{Y} = a + b_1 * X_1 + {\epsilon}_i $$ $$ {\epsilon}_i = \hat{Y} - (a + b_1 * X_1) $$ Error = Actual Scores - Predicted Values ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} SST <- sum((mod1$model$narc_scale - mean(mod1$model$narc_scale))^2) SSM <- sum(mod1$residuals^2) SST ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 32.46321 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} SSM ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 30.81636 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} SST - SSM ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 1.646843 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} (SST - SSM)/SST ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 0.05072953 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} summary(mod1)$r.squared # relative difference ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 0.05072953 ``` ::: ::: ::: ::::: #### To Control How do we use this knowledge?? ::::::::: ## ACTIVITY : In the Vision Board. **Predict narc_score from another variable in the hormone dataset.** - DESCRIBE : your variables. What do you learn? - PREDICT : define the model. Why does it exist? - ERROR : evaluate the error ($R^2$; methods & measures too!) and compare this to the model from the check-in. - CONTROL : how might you use this knowledge? # IF TIME : When Models Get Complicated ## Multiple Regression {.smaller} :::::::::::: panel-tabset #### narcissism \~ sex Sex is related to narcissism... ::::: columns ::: {.column width="60%"} ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} library(gplots) plotmeans(narc_scale ~ sexF, data = h, connect = F) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](3_xeboot_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-8-1.png){width=480} ::: ::: ::: ::: {.column width="40%"} ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} export_summs(mod1, error_format = "", scale = T, transform.response = TRUE) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
Model 1
(Intercept)-0.34 *
     
sexFMale0.49 *
     
N114     
R20.05  
All continuous variables are mean-centered and scaled by 1 standard deviation. *** p < 0.001; ** p < 0.01; * p < 0.05.
``` ::: ::: ::: ::::: #### narcissism \~ test1 Narcissism is also related to testosterone (a hormone). ::::: columns ::: {.column width="60%"} ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} mod2 <- lm(narc_scale ~ test1, data = h) plot(narc_scale ~ test1, data = h) abline(mod2, lwd = 5, col = 'red') ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](3_xeboot_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-10-1.png){width=480} ::: ::: ::: ::: {.column width="40%"} ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} export_summs(mod2, error_format = "", scale = T, transform.response = TRUE) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
Model 1
(Intercept)0.00   
      
test10.28 **
      
N86      
R20.08   
All continuous variables are mean-centered and scaled by 1 standard deviation. *** p < 0.001; ** p < 0.01; * p < 0.05.
``` ::: ::: ::: ::::: #### test1 \~ sexF But if testosterone is related to sex... ::::: columns ::: {.column width="60%"} ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} modx <- lm(test1 ~ sexF, data = h) plotmeans(test1 ~ sexF, data = h, connect = F) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](3_xeboot_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-12-1.png){width=480} ::: ::: ::: ::: {.column width="40%"} ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} export_summs(modx, error_format = "", scale = T, transform.response = TRUE) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
Model 1
(Intercept)-0.94 ***
       
sexFMale1.34 ***
       
N90       
R20.38    
All continuous variables are mean-centered and scaled by 1 standard deviation. *** p < 0.001; ** p < 0.01; * p < 0.05.
``` ::: ::: ::: ::::: #### narcissism \~ sexF + test1 **How are they uniquely related?** ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} mod3 <- lm(narc_scale ~ sexF + test1, data = h) export_summs(mod1, mod2, mod3, error_format = "", scale = T, transform.response = TRUE) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
Model 1Model 2Model 3
(Intercept)-0.34 *0.00   -0.01  
                
sexFMale0.49 *      0.02  
                
test1     0.28 **0.27 *
                
N114     86      86     
R20.05  0.08   0.08  
All continuous variables are mean-centered and scaled by 1 standard deviation. *** p < 0.001; ** p < 0.01; * p < 0.05.
``` ::: ::: :::::::::::: ## Interaction Effects {.smaller} ### What's Going On In This Graph? ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} library(ggthemes) ggplot(data = h, aes(x = test1, y = narc_scale, color = sexF)) + geom_point(size = .5, alpha = .3, position = "jitter") + labs(title = "The Interaction Effect", x = "Testosterone (Time 1)", y = "Narcissism", color = "Sex") + geom_smooth(method = lm) + theme_apa() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x' ``` ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` Warning: Removed 36 rows containing non-finite outside the scale range (`stat_smooth()`). ``` ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` Warning: Removed 36 rows containing missing values or values outside the scale range (`geom_point()`). ``` ::: ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](3_xeboot_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-15-1.png){fig-align='center' width=960} ::: ::: ### Interaction Effect, Explained? ::::: columns ::: {.column width="40%"} ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x' ``` ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` Warning: Removed 36 rows containing non-finite outside the scale range (`stat_smooth()`). ``` ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` Warning: Removed 36 rows containing missing values or values outside the scale range (`geom_point()`). ``` ::: ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](3_xeboot_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-16-1.png){width=672} ::: ::: ::: ::: {.column width="60%"} ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} library(arm) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` Loading required package: MASS ``` ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` Loading required package: Matrix ``` ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` Loading required package: lme4 ``` ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` arm (Version 1.14-4, built: 2024-4-1) ``` ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` Working directory is /Users/lapcat/Documents/Documents - lapcat/catterson.github.io/macssstats/slides ``` ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` Attaching package: 'arm' ``` ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` The following object is masked from 'package:jtools': standardize ``` ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` The following objects are masked from 'package:psych': logit, rescale, sim ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} mod4 <- lm(narc_scale ~ sexF * test1, data = h) mod1z <- standardize(mod1, NULL, TRUE) mod2z <- standardize(mod2, NULL, TRUE) mod3z <- standardize(mod3, NULL, TRUE) mod4z <- standardize(mod4, NULL, TRUE) export_summs(mod1z, mod2z, mod3z, mod4z, error_format = "", scale = T, transform.response = TRUE) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

All continuous variables are mean-centered and scaled by 1 standard deviation. *** p < 0.001; ** p < 0.01; * p < 0.05.
``` ::: ::: ::: ::::: ### More Examples ::: panel-tabset #### resume data Bertrand, M., & Mullainathan, S. (2004). Are Emily and Greg more employable than Lakisha and Jamal? A field experiment on labor market discrimination. American economic review, 94(4), 991-1013. \[[Link](https://www.aeaweb.org/articles?id=10.1257/0002828042002561)\] ![](images/clipboard-1943000841.png) #### puzzle data Hoffman, M., Gneezy, U., & List, J. A. (2011). Nurture affects gender differences in spatial abilities. Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences, 108(36), 14786-14788. \[[Link](https://www.pnas.org/doi/10.1073/pnas.1015182108)\] ![](images/clipboard-4218932613.png) ::: ## ACTIVITY : In the Vision Board **Think about an [*interaction effect*]{.underline} that might be relevant to your Capstone Project \<3** - What is some PATTERN in the data that you expect to see or would be interested to test? - How might some OTHER variable influence this pattern? # PART 2 : Objectivity Interrogations ## RECAP : we (mostly) think it's possible to work toward validity - Anti-Positivism : Attempts to predict people with numbers is bad. - Positivism : We will someday make perfect predictions about people. - Post-Positivism : We will continually improve our predictions, but never get perfect. - Social Constructivism : There is no "truth" about people; we make it up. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} o <- read.csv("~/Dropbox/!GRADSTATS/COMPSS222/Datasets/Class Datasets/MaCSS - Onboarding Data/DATASET_MACSS_onboard_SP26.csv", stringsAsFactors = T) plot(o$epistemology, col = "black", bor = "white", main = "Epistemologies") ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](3_xeboot_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-18-1.png){width=480} ::: ::: ## DISCUSS : Questions Y'all Had :) - how to tell the difference between normal academic criticism of a study and criticism that comes from bias against the researcher? - I'd love to see some interview snippts of white interviewees' answers. \[PROF AGREES : the fact they did not include is an example of low DISCRIMINANT VALIDITY, since would want to see evidence that white people are not talking about these things too.\] - Objectivity armoring...feels like it has a cultural dimension that we can explore in class as well. **\[PROF SAYS : YES!\]** ## DISCUSS : examples from your experiences? anticipation in the future? ![](images/clipboard-2200114898.png) ## NEXT WEEK : Non-Empirical Paper ![](images/clipboard-2030583995.png){fig-align="center" width="80%"} # PART 4 : Likert Scales ## Theory ## Examples in Hormone Dataset ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} plot(test1 ~ test2, data = h) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](3_xeboot_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-19-1.png){width=672} ::: ```{.r .cell-code} test.df <- cbind(h$test1, h$test2) h$TESTAVG <- rowMeans(test.df, na.rm = T) hist(h$TESTAVG, breaks = 20) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](3_xeboot_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-19-2.png){width=672} ::: ::: ## Lab 2 : Working With the Self-Esteem Dataset {.smaller} | Likert Scale Item | Positive or Negative Key Item*"Yes" = High in Self-Esteem = Positive Key.* | Any Issues of Face Validity? | |-----------------------------------|------------------|--------------------| | Q1. I feel that I am a person of worth, at least on an equal plane with others. | | | | Q2. I feel that I have a number of good qualities. | | | | Q3. All in all, I am inclined to feel that I am a failure. | | | | Q4. I am able to do things as well as most other people. | | | | Q5. I feel I do not have much to be proud of. | | | | Q6. I take a positive attitude toward myself. | | | | Q7. On the whole, I am satisfied with myself. | | | | Q8. I wish I could have more respect for myself. | | | | Q9. I certainly feel useless at times. | | | | Q10. At times I think I am no good at all. | | | # [THE END : CHECK-OUT!](https://docs.google.com/forms/d/e/1FAIpQLSd142WkIOaFZHO2nS4L5uOBqH-13h206qHgcfn6qgVCoXeLOg/viewform?usp=publish-editor)
